title: What digital twins taught me about building futures
summary: A reflection on digital twins and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: digital twins after the magic show
body: My notes on digital twins began with a small irritation in January 2024. The speed with which people agreed on the big story was the part that unsettled me first. The quicker the consensus, the more likely it is that someone simplified the story past the point of honesty. In this case the omitted piece was how trust is built from boring details. What sounds abstract from altitude becomes concrete very quickly once you ask who maintains the system, who absorbs failure, and who benefits from early trust.

Digital twins appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. Talking about the engineering is straightforward because it collapses cleanly into diagrams, benchmarks, and staged proof. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool drags along a picture of its preferred user and a social setting it would like to stabilize. If you pay attention, those assumptions are right there in the design. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the kinds of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly teaches while it works.

The real split, to my eye, was between prototype logic and public-life conditions. One virtue of prototype life is that everyone knows the terms are still experimental. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. A tool behaves very differently when it is tried first on people who opted in for the strange version. It is one thing among volunteers and another inside systems people cannot casually opt out of. How trust is built from boring details becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

I remained interested in the subject, just less willing to let abstract language skate by. After writing “A note on image models while the hype still outran the evidence,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. Any claim about the future that collapses on contact with ordinary life does not earn much trust from me. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. Still, it matters to notice when a useful tool crosses the line into quiet obligation. Digital twins seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Usually it arrives as a relationship pattern that turns ordinary before anyone has really consented to it.

A final note to myself from January 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The winner of the argument interests me less than the people expected to absorb the system every day. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: A note on image models while the hype still outran the evidence
On coordination systems when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
